did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a novel Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) to classify melanoma (malignant) vs. benign skin lesions using dermoscopic images from the ISIC 2016, 2017, and 2020 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN/LCNet) for melanoma classification. It details the design of the network, describes the methodology using ISIC 2016, 2017, and 2020 datasets, and reports new performance results (accuracy, precision, recall, etc.) comparing the proposed model against state-of-the-art methods. The abstract explicitly states 'This research proposes an automated melanoma classifier' and the conclusion summarizes experimental findings, indicating it is an original technical study rather than a review.

title: Melanoma Classification Using a Novel Deep Convolutional Neural Network with Dermoscopic Images
brief_summary: This study proposes a novel lightweight Deep Convolutional Neural Network (LCNet) to automate the binary classification of malignant melanoma versus benign skin lesions using dermoscopic images. The model was trained and evaluated on datasets from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC 2016, 2017, and 2020) as well as the PH 2 dataset. The primary evaluation approach involved assessing performance metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, specificity, and F1-score against state-of-the-art pre-trained networks like ResNet18 and Inceptionv3. The proposed LCNet achieved accuracies of 81.41%, 88.23%, and 90.42% on the ISIC 2016, 2017, and 2020 datasets, respectively. These results demonstrate that the lightweight architecture offers high efficiency and superior performance compared to existing methods, particularly on larger, more balanced datasets like ISIC 2020. The authors conclude that this approach provides a less complex and advanced framework for expediting melanoma diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for the binary classification task (malignant vs. benign). Included in ISIC 2016, 2017, 2020, and PH 2 datasets. Subtypes mentioned in literature context: acral, nodular, superficial, lentigo.

The comparator class in the binary classification task. Aggregated label used in ISIC 2016, 2020, and PH 2 datasets. In ISIC 2017, this group specifically includes Nevus (NV) and Seborrheic Keratosis (SK).

A specific lesion type present in the ISIC 2017 dataset, grouped under the 'BEN' (Benign) label for the binary classification experiment. Described as having distinct edges, appearing on arms, legs, trunk in pink, brown, tan colors.

A specific lesion type present in the ISIC 2017 dataset (listed as BKL in dataset description, SK in lesion type description), grouped under the 'BEN' (Benign) label for the binary classification experiment. Described as non-cancerous, waxy brown, black, or tan.
Mentioned in the introduction as one of the three main types of skin cancer, but not used as a target class in the experiments.
Mentioned in the introduction as one of the three main types of skin cancer, but not used as a target class in the experiments.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of malignant melanoma (MEL) versus benign skin lesions (BEN, including Nevus and Seborrheic Keratosis).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the objective is to 'classify malignant vs. benign melanoma' and 'identify and distinguish between MEL and BEN cancers'. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (MEL vs BEN) using dermoscopic images from ISIC 2016, 2017, and 2020 datasets. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, specificity, and F1-score. The task is binary classification, although the authors note the model could be extended to multi-class classification in the future.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2016
datasets_used.description: Dermoscopic image dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration challenge, augmented with additional samples from the ISIC archive to balance classes.

use.validation: Hyperparameter tuning and performance monitoring (10% of samples)

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: Dermoscopic image dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration challenge, containing original samples plus additional samples from the ISIC archive to balance classes. Includes specific lesion types NV and SK grouped as BEN.

use.validation: Hyperparameter tuning and performance monitoring (10% of samples)

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2020
datasets_used.description: Dermoscopic image dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration challenge, used for binary classification of MEL vs BEN.

use.validation: Hyperparameter tuning and performance monitoring (10% of samples)

datasets_used.name: PH 2
datasets_used.description: Dataset from the Dermatology Service of Hospital Pedro Hispano, Matosinhos, Portugal, used for external testing.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To divide datasets into subsets for model training, hyperparameter tuning, and final performance evaluation.
To address class imbalance and prevent bias in lesion samples, specifically noted to improve performance on the imbalanced ISIC 2017 dataset.
To identify and eliminate redundant or identical image samples during data normalization (threshold > 0.99).
To measure the error between prediction scores and target labels during network training.
As the learning algorithm for optimizing network weights during training.

statistical_methods_used.method: Comparison of classification metrics (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, Specificity, F1-Score)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate and compare the performance of the proposed LCNet model against state-of-the-art methods (ResNet18, Inceptionv3, AlexNet) and the impact of oversampling.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, specificity, F1-score) and compared against state-of-the-art models without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2016.metric: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, Specificity, F1-Score, AUC, Inference Time
ISIC 2016.notes: Inference time on 344 test images was 3.77 s. The model converged after 80 epochs. LCNet precision was the highest among compared state-of-the-art methods.

ISIC 2017.metric: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, Specificity, F1-Score, AUC, Inference Time
ISIC 2017.notes: Inference time on 835 test images was 15.7 s. The original dataset was highly imbalanced, leading to poor results without oversampling. Only Accuracy and Specificity of reference [46] were higher than the proposed model; LCNet had the highest Precision among all studies.

ISIC 2020.metric: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, Specificity, F1-Score, AUC, Inference Time
ISIC 2020.notes: Inference time on 2014 test images was 61.6 s. Higher performance was noticed due to the large number of samples. The method performed better on this large and balanced dataset compared to ISIC 2016 and 2017.

PH 2.notes: Used as an external testing dataset. No specific comparison metrics or AUC values were reported for this dataset in the text.

architecture_used.input_size: Rescaled to lower dimensions (exact pixel dimensions not specified)

architecture_details.block_structure: Multiple blocks repeated in a ratio of 2:4:2
architecture_details.feature_aggregation: Concatenation of features from each block to pass information to the next

training_details.data_augmentation: Random oversampling, rotation, translation, and scaling

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layer followed by Softmax
classification_or_output_head.function: Calculates confidence score/probability for class categories

preprocessing_techniques: Data normalization to eliminate redundancy: conversion to grayscale, histogram calculation, mean determination, and correlation index calculation (threshold > 0.99) to discard identical images.
Cropping to locate lesions in the center and transform images into square format.
Resizing/Scaling: Rescaling images to 128 × 128 dimensions using bilinear interpolation while preserving aspect ratio.
Data augmentation (applied only to training sets): Random oversampling, rotation (-300 to +300), scaling (0.8 in X-direction, 1.0 in Y-direction), and translation (-5 and +5).
No explicit noise removal (hairlines, gel bubbles, ruler/ink marks) was applied as the DCNN processes raw images with artifacts.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to underrepresented classes (e.g., MEL in ISIC 2016, BEN in ISIC 2017/2020) to balance class distribution by generating more artificial samples. Applied only to training sets.
Online data augmentation applied during training with a range of -300 to +300 degrees.
Online data augmentation applied during training with factors of 0.8 in the X-direction and 1.0 in the Y-direction.
Online data augmentation applied during training with shifts of -5 and +5.

conclusions: The proposed lightweight Deep Convolutional Neural Network (LCNet) achieved high performance in binary classification of malignant melanoma versus benign skin lesions, with accuracies of 81.41% on ISIC 2016, 88.23% on ISIC 2017, and 90.42% on ISIC 2020.

LCNet demonstrated superior performance compared to state-of-the-art pre-trained networks (ResNet18, Inceptionv3, AlexNet) and existing literature methods, particularly in precision, while maintaining a lower number of learnable parameters (3.3M).
The model's performance was significantly improved by data oversampling, which was crucial for handling the class imbalance present in the ISIC 2017 dataset.
LCNet performed best on the large and balanced ISIC 2020 dataset, indicating that the deep learning-based model requires large datasets for efficient training and optimal performance.

The proposed method offers a less complex and advanced framework for automating melanoma diagnosis, with low inference time (0.1 s per image) and high processing speed, making it suitable for clinical decision support.
The study acknowledges that the designed DCNN model can be further extended to multi-class classification to predict other different types of skin cancers.
important_remarks: The study lacks formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) to validate the significance of performance differences between the proposed LCNet and state-of-the-art baselines.
The model was trained from scratch without pre-training on ImageNet or other large-scale datasets, which may limit its feature extraction capabilities compared to transfer learning approaches.
Performance on the external PH 2 dataset (76.0% accuracy) was notably lower than on ISIC datasets (81-90%), suggesting potential dataset bias or domain shift issues that limit generalizability.

The paper acknowledges that the model performs best on large, balanced datasets (ISIC 2020) and struggles with smaller or imbalanced datasets (ISIC 2016/2017) without oversampling, indicating a dependency on data volume and balance.
Data leakage risk exists due to the use of additional samples from the ISIC archive to balance classes, as the specific provenance and overlap with standard test splits are not fully detailed.

No explicit preprocessing was applied to remove common dermoscopic artifacts (hairlines, gel bubbles, ruler marks), relying instead on the network's ability to process raw images, which may affect robustness in clinical settings.

The study is limited to binary classification (Melanoma vs. Benign) and does not address multi-class differentiation among specific benign lesion types (e.g., Nevus vs. Seborrheic Keratosis), which is clinically relevant for differential diagnosis.
The authors declare no external funding and no conflicts of interest, but the reliance on publicly available ISIC datasets limits the novelty of the data contribution.
